text: BE'ZIERS, an arrondissement, canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Herault.-The arrond. comprises an area of 180,195 hectares; and contains 11 cant.; viz., Agde, Bedarieux, B., Capestang, Florensac, Saint-Gervais, Montagnac, Murviel, Pezenas, Boujan, and Servian. Its pop. in 1831 was estimated at 123,647; in 1835, at 128,149; and in 1846, at 131,033.—The cant. comprises 16 com., and contained in 1831 a pop. of 29,661.—The town is beautifully situated on the l. bank of the Orbe, at the junction of the canal of Languedoc with that river, at an alt. of 370 ft. above sea-level, 6 m. from the shore of the Mediterranean, and 40 m. SW of Montpellier. It is surrounded by an ancient wall flanked with towers; and contains a fine Gothic church, which was formerly a cathedral, and several parish churches, a communal college, a public library, and a theatre. It has also a chamber-of-commerce, and an agricultural association; and possesses manufactories of silkstockings, gloves, parchment, starch, verdigris, and other chemical productions, and sweetmeats, tanneries, distilleries, 4 printing-establishments, and a glass-house. The commerce of B. consists chiefly in articles of local produce, especially brandy and other spirituous liquors, and wines, grain, fruit, meal, silk, BHA wool, leather, and oil. The surrounding district is extremely fertile, and is celebrated for the salubrity of its climate. It affords coal in considerable quantities. Pop. in 1789, 12,896; in 1821, 16,140; in 1831, 16,769; in 1836, 16,233; in 1846, 18,871. About 3 m. from the town is the tunnel of Malpa, cut through a mountain for the passage of the canal. -B. is of great antiquity, and still retains some architectural remains of its former importance. Previous to the conquest of the Romans it bore the name of Biterræ Septimanorum. It was, to a great extent, destroyed by the Goths in the 5th cent., and again in 737, it was captured, and almost entirely demolished, by Charles Martel. It gradually rose again to importance; and, in the reign of Charlemagne, became extremely flourishing. During the war of the Albigenses, in 1209, it was taken by assault, and 10,000 of its inhabitants put to the sword.
BEZLIOUDOVKA, a town of Russia in Europe, in the gov. and 9 m. S of Kharkov, on the l. bank of the Oudi.
BEZMICHOW, a village of Galicia, in the circle and 10 m. ESE of Sanok, near the r. bank of the San. It has sulphur works, and a vitriol manufactory.

BEZOARA, or BIJORA, a town of Hindostan, in the prov. of the northern Circars, on the l. bank of the Kistnah, 40 m. NW of Masulipatam. It contains a well-built Mahommedan serai and mosque, and a finely situated bungalow for the accommodation of travellers. In the environs are numerous temples excavated in the rocks; but they are greatly inferior in execution to those of the western districts of India. The pop. is composed chiefly of Brahmins. The Kistnah-which is here contracted by two steep mountain-ridges to a width of only 100 yardsforms the ferry of the great thoroughfare between the provinces of the Circars and Carnatic. The passage is effected by large deep boats and catamarans.

BEZONS, a commune of France, in the dep. of Seine-et-Oise, and cant. of Argenteuil, on the r. bank of the Seine, which is here crossed by a fine bridge, 10 m. N of Versailles. It has an annual fair, which is the most frequented in the vicinity of Paris. The village of B. lays claim to great antiquity.

BEZONZOUS, or ANCAYE, a tribe of the island of Madagascar, who inhabit a small district near the centre of the island between the territories of the Ancova and Betaminenes. Their villages, which are numerous, are generally situated on eminences, and surrounded by ditches and low parapet walls, flanked with towers and bastions, beyond which are esplanades enclosed with palisades, and commonly used as pasture-ground for their cattle.
BHADAR, or BHADRA, a river of India, in Guzerat, rising in Mount Mandawa, and flowing S to the sea near Nairbender, which it enters after a course of nearly 100 m.
BHADRACALAM. See BADRACHILLUM.
BHADRINATH. See BADRINATH.
BHAGAHKITANDA, a village of Sinde, 60 m. SE of Hyderabad, near the l. bank of the Gumi.
BHAGELPUR. See BHAUGULPORE.

BHAGHIRETTI, or BAGHIRATI, the western headstream of the Ganges, rising in the Himalaya, and flowing SSW through a narrow valley, then turning SE, and after passing Tiri in Gurwal, joining the Alakananda at Deoprang.
BHAGMATI, a river of India, in the district of Tirhut, in Bahar, flowing into the Ganges.

BHANMO, or BHAMMO, a town of Pegu, in N lat. 24° 7', E long. 97° 15', 12 or 15 m. W of the Chinese frontier, near the junction of the Pinlang with the Irawaddi. An annual caravan arrives here, in the month of December, from China, bringing copper, orpiment, quicksilver, vermilion, iron, tin, lead, alum, silver, gold, earthenware, carpets, rhubarb, tea, honey, silk, velvets, spirits, paper, umbrellas, shoes, dogs, and pheasants; and China takes in return raw cotton, feathers, edible nests, ivory, sapphires, serpentine, and a small quantity of British made woollens.
BHATGONG, or BHATGAONG, a town of Nepal Proper, in Hindostan, in N lat. 27° 40', E long. 85° 8', 8 m. ESE of Catmandu. Pop. 12,000.
BHAROUCH. See BAROACH.
BHATNIR. See BHUTNIR.
BHATTAH, or Batta, sometimes Bandhu, a district of India, in the prov. of Gundwana, now comprised in the collectorship of Allahabad.
BHATTI, a town of India, in Lahore, in the Beit Jalindar.

BHATTIPOH, a ruined town of India, in Rajastan, 6 m. SE of Bhakar, once the cap. of the Sumaras, and last held by the Bhattis. BHATTIS, BHATS, or BHATIYAS, a tribe in Rajputana and the Punjab, of predatory habits, and professing Mahommedanism. They chiefly inhabit the country lying between the Sutledge and the Beyah on the N, and the Bikanir territory on the S; and bounded by the Hurriana district of Delhi on the E, and the Great Sandy desert on the W.
BHAUG. See BAGH.

BHAUGULPORE, or BOGLIPUR, a district of Hindostan, in the provinces of Bahar and Bengal. It lies along both sides of the Ganges, between N lat. 23° and 25°; and is bounded on the N by Tirhut and Purneah; on the E by Purneah and Murshedabad; on the S by Birbhum; and on the W by Bahar and Ramgur. Its area is 8,225 sq. m. Pop. 2,020,000, of whom two-thirds are Hindus. Upwards of onethird of the surface is covered with forests and thickets. Rice, wheat, barley, maize, sugar, and potatoes, are cultivated for food; and some cotton and indigo are grown. A few wild elephants roam in the forests. The land-revenue in 1829-30 was only £45,760. -The principal towns are the cap., of the same name; and the towns of Champanagar, Monger, Cholgang, and Rajmahal.

BHAUGULPORE, the cap. of the above district, is in N lat. 25° 13′, 110 m. NW of Murshedabad, 240 m. NW of Calcutta. It has a pop. of about 30,000, who are chiefly Mahommedans. It is the residence of a British magistrate, and is reckoned one of the healthiest stations in India.
BHAVANI, a river of India, in the prov. of Coimbatur, which joins the Caveri near the town of Bhavani Kudal, 60 m. NE of Coimbatur.
BHAVANI-KUDAL, a well-built town of India, in the prov. of Coimbatur, in N lat. 11° 26', E long. 77° 44'. It is situated in a low fertile district, to the SE of the Bhavani river.

BHAVANI, or BHAWANI, a town of India, in the district of Hurriana, in the prov. of Delhi, in N lat. 28° 37'. Its inhabitants effectively resisted all attempts of the native chiefs to impose tribute upon them; but finally submitted to British authority after a well sustained resistance. BHAWULPUR, BHAWLPUR, OF BUHAWULPUR, a small state and town of Rajasthan, on the confines of the Great Indian desert, and comprising a portion of the level country of Sind. The district which extends along the Gharra, is nearly 300 m. in length from NE to SW. Its greatest breadth is 110 m. Much of it is mere desert; but some portions are well-cultivated and fertile. Wheat, rice, indigo, sugar, cotton, opium, and various kinds of fruits are grown. The wild animals are tigers, hogs, deer, aquatic fowl, and game. The inhabitants are Jats, Beluchis, and Afghans. The khan professes Mahommedanism, and has an annual revenue of about 1,500,000 rupees. The principal towns are Bhawulpur, the cap., Ahmedpur, Ooch, and Khanpur.—The town of B. lies to the NE of Ahmedpur, in N lat. 29° 17'. It contains about 8,000 houses, with two large bazaars. It is surrounded by a low mud wall, but has no fort, and is far from imposing in appearance. The BHO principal manufactures of the place are silk goods and chintzes. Mangoes, grapes, peaches, almonds, apples, figs, pomegranates, and other fruits, are grown in the adjoining gardens. The Gharra river flows about 2 m. to the NW of the city, with a full muddy stream nearly half-a-mile wide, when seen by Lieut. Trevelyan in the early part of May. The desert comes up to within 3 m. of the SE side of the city. The higher and probably military portion of the community, dress in loose, flowing, and graceful robes; while the labouring part are distinguished by their dark blue dhotees. Many dye their hair red; and the lower orders are peculiarly addicted to snuff-taking. BHEELS, BHILS, or BHILLAS, a rude mountaineer race inhabiting the defiles and fastnesses of Candeish, in the Indian territory of the Dekkan, and the wild uncultivated tracts stretching along the Nerbudda from Nemar to Gujerat. With the exception of a few who have embraced Mahommedanism, they profess the Brahminical faith; they also pay a peculiar kind of aderation to their deceased chiefs. They divide themselves into two great castes, the Ujwala, or 'White,' i.e. pure, - and the Kala, or 'Black,' i. e. impure. The former appear to be the descendants of degraded Rajputs. Unlike the Hindus, they always bury their dead. Their villages are clusters of rude huts, several of which are commonly united under the command of one chief. Heber says that the Rajputs allow that most of their cities and fortresses were founded by such or such B. chiefs; and that the B. are generally regarded by British officers as "a better race than their conquerors." Their word is more to be depended on. They are of a franker and more hospitable character; and their women are better treated, and exercise a humaner influence throughout their society.
BHEEMTHAL. See BEEMTHAL.

BHILSA, or BILSAH, a district and town of Hindostan, in the prov. of Malwa. The town is in N lat. 23° 33′, 30 m. NE of Bopal, on the Betwah. It contained 5,000 houses in 1820. The district is celebrated for the fine tobacco which is cultivated in it.

BHILWARA, a large commercial town of Rajasthan, in Marwar, 20 m. NE of Pali. Under the direction of Colonel Tod this place, which had fallen into a state of entire decadence, has sprung into a new and vigorous existence. When Heber visited it in 1826, he found it exhibiting "a greater appearance of trade, industry, and moderate but widely diffused wealth and comfort, than he had seen since he left Delhi."
BHIMA, a river of India, in Bijapur. It rises about 40 m. N of Punah, and flows in a prevailingly SE course to the Krishna, which it joins near Firozegur, after a course of 400 m.

BHIMBER, or BHIMBUR, a village of India, in the Punjab, on an affluent of the Chenab, at the extremity of a defile of the same name, leading in a SW direction through the Pir Panjal into Cashmere. The first ghat, or summit of a ridge, is about 8 m. from Bhimber in a direct line, and about 1,000 ft. above the level of the Punjab. [Vigne.] Iron is mined in the neighbourhood of this place.
BHINMAL. See BEENMAHL.
BHIRKOT, a small Nepaulese state, in N lat. 27" 58', E long. 83° 48'.
BHIROWAL, a town of India, in the prov. of Lahore, in N lat. 31° 20'. It is on the N side of the Beyah, which is here 740 yards wide in the rainy season.
BHOBANESER, or BHAVANESER, a ruined city of Hindostan, in the prov. of Orissa, 16 m. from Cuttak, near Bulwanta.
BHOLAN. See BOLAN.
BHONSEA, a district of Hindostan, in the Coucan, lying between the Dewghur river on the N, and the territory of Goa on the S. Its chief town is Warri or Sawuntwarri.

BHOOMLUNGTUNG, a village of Butan, in India, in a valley between the N extremity of the Rodula pass and the town of Tongsa. It is situated in a valley, occupied in 1837-8 chiefly by wheat-fields, "clean and even coarsely manured," and in a few instances surrounded by stone walls, at an elevation of 8,700 ft. above sea-level.—Pemberton.
BHOPARI, a fort in the district of Berdi, in the prov. of Gundwana, in Hindostan.
BHOPAUL. See BOPAL.

BHOREGHAUT, a pass in India, between the maritime district of Callianee and the table-land of the Deccan, on the road from Bombay to Poonah, in the prov. of Aurungabad.—Also a pass in the mountains of Junir, in the same prov., conducting to the tableland on which the city of Poonah is built. The former is called the Greater B.; the latter, the Less B.
BHOWANIPUR, a town of India, in the district of Dinagipur, in Bengal, at which a great Mahommedan festival is held in the month of April.
BHOWNUGGUR, a seaport of India, in the prov. of Gujerat, on the W side of the gulf of Cambay, in N lat. 21° 48'. It has a bad celebrity for its mint of forged rupees.
BHRADINATH. See BADRINATH.

BHUJ, the capital of the prov. of Cutch, in the SW extremity of Hindostan, in N lat. 23° 15'. It occupies a rising ground about 25 m. to the NW of the port of Muddi. In 1818 this town contained 20,000 inhabitants; but in the following year, after being captured by the British, both town and fort were nearly destroyed by an earthquake.
BHUJI, a district of Hindostan, in the prov. of Delhi, between the Jumna and the Sutledge, in which the fortress of Kungur is situated.
BHUR, a village of Sinde, 80 m. S of Hyderabad, in N lat. 24° 11'.

BHURTPORE, a principality of Hindostan, in the prov. of Agra, on its W boundary. The district has an area of about 5,000 sq. m., and is well-cultivated. Its principal productions are corn, cotton, and sugar. It is chiefly inhabited by a tribe of Jats, who migrated hither from the banks of the Indus in the early part of the 17th cent. Its chief towns are B., Dieg, Combher, Biana, and Karna.—The town and fort of B. were erected by Budden Singh, a descendant of Churamun, a Jat chief, who acquired considerable power during the civil wars between Aurungzebe and his brothers, and enriched himself and followers by plundering the stragglers of the different armies, and laying the merchants under contribution. His successors extended their usurpations nearly as far as Delhi; and during the various vicissitudes of that court, their alliance was sought by the contending parties. On the breaking out of the Mahratta war, Runjeet Singh, the rajah of B., promised to join the British, but afterwards formed an alliance with Scindia and Holkar, in consequence of which its territories were invaded by the British troops under Lake, and the fortress of Dieg and several other places taken; but the garrison of B. defended themselves with such gallantry, that 3,100 of the besieging army fell before the place capitulated. In 1825, Bulwunt Singh, an infant of seven years of age, was recognised by the British as rajah of B. His cousin, Durjun Sal, managed to usurp the sovereignty of the state; and a British force was again employed in reducing B., which was only effected in January 1826, after the garrison had lost above 4,000 men. The fortifications were then destroyed; but the independence of the state has been guaranteed by the Company's government.

BHUTANT, or BHOTE, a mountain-district of Hindostan, on the S boundary of Tibet, through which the passes from Gurwal and Kumaon lead into Tibet. A great portion of this district—now attached to Gurwal and Kumaon - is covered with perpetual snow, in consequence of its vast elevation. The only grains that come to maturity are a species of wheat called papira, and some kinds of barley. Spring BIA commences in June. The pop., which amounts to about 10,000, consists of Bhutias or Bhotyas, Tibetians, and a few people said to be of Tartar blood. This district is to be distinguished from Butan, the territory of the Deb rajah.

BHUTKAK, a large village of Afghanistan, "the last occurring on the plains of Kabul to the E, beyond it commencing the hilly country extending to Jelalabad." It is memorable in the traditions of the country as the place where Sultan Mahmud broke up the idols of Somnath.—Masson.
BHUTKOT, a mountain of Hindostan, in the prov. of Delhi, 20 m. NW of Almirah, having an alt. of 9,200 ft. above sea-level.
BHUTNIR, or BATNIR, a district of Rajisthan, in India, dependent on Bikanir. Its cap., of the same name, is in N lat. 29° 37'.
BIA, a river of Siberia, in the gov. of Tomsk, which issues from Lake Teletzk or Altin, curves to the NW, and unites with the Obi 12 m. SW of Bisk.
BIACHE-SAINT-WAAST, a commune of France, in the dep. of Pas-de-Calais, and cant. of Vitry. Pop. 1,068.

BIAFARAS, a tribe of Africa, inhabiting a district between 11° and 12° N lat., and 13° to 14° 30' W long., on the N of the Rio Grande. It appears that they formerly possessed extensive territories to the SW, and in particular the island of Bulama. From these they were expelled by their warlike neighbours towards the end of the 17th cent., when they retired further up the Rio Grande. In stature the B. are tall, but of a slender feminine figure, unlike the strong and robust natives of most other parts of Africa. They are a mild, peaceable, and inoffensive race; and are held in great contempt by the Bijugas, with whom they are constantly at war. They are of a lively disposition, have a wonderful propensity to talking, and seem to be endowed with a ready apprehension of things within the limits of their understanding. They are governed by different chiefs, and have several towns, among which are Goli, Gonfode, Ghinala, and Bulola. The B. are said to trade to some extent with the Portuguese. They brought ivory, cloths, and poultry to the English settlement, and were extremely desirous that Captain Beaver should establish himself among them.—Beaver's African Memoranda.—Lajaille, Voyage au Senegal, par Labarthe.—Durand's Voyage to Senegal.

BIAFRA (BIGHT OF), a portion of the gulf of Guinea, on the W coast of Africa, lying between Cape Formosa, which divides it from the bight of Benin, on the N, and Cape Lopez on the S. The direct distance between these extreme points is about 580 m., and the length of the coast-line nearly 800 m. The greatest depth of the bay, measured from the line passing betwixt its extreme points to the mouth of the Old Calabar river, is about 255 m. The shores throughout are flat and low; but high hills or mountains rise at a distance inland. The prevalent winds are from SSW, WSW, and W, and the current runs generally to the eastward; but tornadoes occasionally blow from the NE or SE. The water shoals rapidly inshore of 5 fath. Beginning at the NW point of the coast, we have six rivers—all of which may be regarded as deltoid branches of the Niger or Quorra, at least during the rainy season—successively discharging themselves into the sea. These are the Rio Nun; the Rio Bento or St. John, 11 m. to the E of the Rio Nun; the San Nicolao, 11 m. to the E of the Rio Bento; the Santa Barbara, 11 m. ENE from the San Nicolao; the San Bartolomeo, 7½ m. to the E of the Santa Barbara; and the Rio Sombreiro, 11 m. ESE of the San Bartolomeo. All the coast thus far presents a sandy beach thickly covered with mangrove trees. At 8 m. ESE from the Rio Sombreiro is Foche point, the W point of the large estuary of the New Calabar, in N lat. 4° 22′ 40", E long. 7°. This estuary is 8 m. in breadth, and is divided by a large bank in the middle, on several points of which are only 3 ft. water, while a deep channel on the W side leads into the New Calabar, and another on the E into the Bonny. From the Bonny to the entrance of the Andoney or Adony, the land continues low, and trends E by N for 12 m. The latter river is said to communicate by an interior branch with the Bonny, and thus forms what is called Bonny island. From the Andoney to the estuary of the Old Calabar, the shore trends E by N 20 leagues. The basin of the Old Calabar appears to be completely separated from that of the Quorra by high lands running into the interior. Becroft, who ascended this river above 300 m., is of opinion that it is not connected with the water-system of the Quorra, but the Cross river, which falls into its estuary at a point about 60 m. from the sea, comes from the NW, and may yet be found to be connected with the Quorra. From the E side of the estuary of the Old Calabar to the Rio del Rey, the coast-line runs E 12 m.; and from the Rio del Rey to the river Bimbia it trends S 40 m. Here the elevated range of the Camaroons becomes visible, rising in a continuous slope from the sea to an alt. of 13,000 ft., and covered with forests nearly to the summit. The bay of Amboises lies at the base of the Camaroons, and opposite the NE point of Fernando Po, the largest island in the bight. The channel between this island and the main is very picturesque, being only 19 m. wide; and having on the one hand the lofty Camaroons, and, on the other, the central peak of the island, which attains an alt. of 10,190 ft. The coast of the main is here thickly studded with villages. From the Bimbia to Cape Camaroons, on the W side of the estuary of that river, the coast forms a sweep of 11 m. Captain Allen reached the main undivided trunk of the Camaroons river at a point about 20 m. from the sea. Its average breadth as far as he ascended was about 400 yards, and the depth varied from 2 to 20 ft., but rarely exceeded 8 ft. When flooded there would be water enough for any vessel; but the natives reported that the navigation was obstructed by rocks at Banem, about 90 m. from the sea. From the E side of the estuary of the Camaroons, all the way S to Corisco bay, the coast presents a continued range of low banks thickly covered with trees, amidst which numerous villages appear embosomed. The principal rivers which discharge themselves on this part of the coast are the Borea, the Campo, and the Bento. Cape Esterias, the S point of Corisco bay, is in N lat. 0° 37', E long. 9° 21', and from this point to Cape Clara, at the entrance of the Gabun river, the coast trends S 1/2 E 7 m. The estuary of the Gabun is 18 m. wide, and it widens inland to above 30 m. From the Gabun to Cape Lopez, the coast forms a bay measuring 22 leagues across, and 14 m. in depth, with several rivers and creeks opening from it. Near the Gabun, the interior rises gradually into rich fertile hills, and is thickly wooded to the water's edge; towards the cape, the coast-line becomes so low that the trees and bushes seem to stand in the water. Little is known of the states and tribes of the interior of this part of Africa. There appears to be no great controlling state amongst them; but the productions are rich, and the country is in many parts populous.- Bowdich. -Allen,-Lander.-Vidul.-Geog. Journal.
BIAGIO (SAN), a town of Venetian Lombardy, in the district and 7 m. E of Treviso. - Also a town of Naples, in the prov. of Samnio, 10 m. N of Campobasso.

BIAGIO (SAN), or SAN BIASI, a town of Naples, in the prov. of Calabria Ultra, in the dist. and 4 m. WSW of Nicastro. It was, to a great extent, overthrown by an earthquake in 1783. The environs afford excellent wine.
BIAGRASSO. See ABBIATEGRASSO.

BIALA, a town of Galicia, in the circle and 20 m. WSW of Wadowice, on the r. bank of the river of the same name, 7 m. S of its confluence with the Vistula, and opposite the Moravian town of Bielitz. with which it is connected by a bridge. It possesses manufactories of cloth, linen, and ironmongery. It was constituted a free town in 1797. Pop. 3,994.—Also a river which takes its rise in the Carpathian mountains, runs to the N, passing Ciezkowice, and unites with the Dojanetz 4 m. WNW of Tarnov.—Also a town of Poland, in the gov. of Mazovia, obwod and 9 m. NE of Rawa. Pop. 820.—Also a town in the gov. and 38 m. ESE of Seidlee, near the N bank of the Krzna. It contains a fine castle, belonging to the family of Radziwill. Pop. 3,600.—Also a town in the gov. of Lublin, and obwod of Zamosc. Pop. 700.
BIALACZOW, a town of Poland, in the gov. of Sandomir, obwod and 5 m. S of Opoczno. Pop. 700.
BIALLA, a town of Prussia, in the prov. of East Prussia, in the circle and 10 m. E of Johannisberg, on a small lake. Pop. in 1837, 1,213. Lint is cultivated in the environs.
BIALLIS, a town of Lower Egypt, in the prov. of Damietta, on the r. side of the Tabanieh canal, 12 m. NNE of Mahalet.
BIALOBRZEGI, a town of Poland, in the gov. of Sandomir, in the obwod and 20 m. NW of Radom, on the r. bank of the Pilica. Pop. 580.
BIALOGROD. See BELGARD.
BIALOLEKA, a village of Poland, in the gov. of Mazovia, in the obwod and 8 m. from Warsaw.
BIALOSTOK. See BIALYSTOCK.

BIALOWIEZ (Forest of), an immense primitween the river Bug and the town of Isla. It is 195 m. in length, and 167 m. in its greatest breadth; and its circuit probably exceeds 700 m. It is watered by three rivers, the Narwa, the Narewka, and the Bialowiczonka. The soil is in general marshy. Among the wild animals inhabiting it are wolves, boars, bears, and the zubr or auroch, supposed by some na- BIA turalists to be the original type of our horned cattle, and which is now only found here, and in the marshy forests of the Caucasus. The chase of the auroch in his native forest formed the most magnificent pastime of the kings of Poland; and a pyramidal obelisk, standing in the centre of this forest, and bearing the date 1752, commemorates a grand chase by Augustus III., when no fewer than 42 of these noble animals were killed. Another chase took place in 1822 for the gratification of the emperor Alexander of Russia; and in 1836 the Prince Dolgoroucki, governor-general of Lithuania, held a grand chase, in which one auroch was allowed to be killed. During the struggle for national independence in 1831, the forest of B. afforded for some time a retreat to the patriots of Grodno.

BIALYKAMIEN, a town of Galicia, in the circle and 8 m. NNW of Zloczow, near the source of the Bug. It forms a seignory belonging to Prince Radziwill, and contains a fine castle, a Catholic and a Greek church.

BIALYSTOCK, a province of Western Russia, which formerly constituted a part of the Polish waivodeship of Podlachia, but was incorporated with Russia in 1795. In 1807, a large portion of it was incorporated with the duchy of Warsaw, in terms of the treaty of Tilsit; and the remainder was erected into the prov. we are now to describe, which was placed under the superintendence of the governors-general of Grodno, and divided into 4 districts, named from the principal towns, Bialystock, Sokolka, Bielsk, and Drohiczyn. Its superficial extent has been estimated by Wiehmann, in 1813, at 158 German or 3,355 English sq. m.; and by Broemsen, in 1819, at 169 German or 3,589 English sq. m. The pop. in 1839 amounted to 227,106, of whom 31,376 were Jews. The greater part of this pop. are Catholics, of Polish-Mazovian descent. The surface is generally flat; and the soil light and sandy. Upwards of a seventh part of the surface is covered with forests. The principal river is the Western Bug. Its tributaries, the Nurzek and the Narawa, are considerable streams. The climate is temperate; but the great extent of marshy ground renders it, in many parts, unhealthy. Rye, wheat, buck-wheat, and flax are extensively grown; and the number of individuals engaged in agriculture was returned in 1830 at 198,502, or nearly five-sixths of the whole pop. Game is plentiful; and wolves and wild boars haunt the larger forests. Tallow, wool, wax, and honey are exported; but the only manufactures are confined to leather, soap, and woollen fabrics for domestic consumption. Upwards of 9,000 families in this prov. claim to be of noble descent; but they are in general so poor that not a few of them cultivate the ground with their own hands, or even sell their bodily services to others for hire.- Drohiczyn, on the Bug, was the ancient cap. of this country; but successively devastated by the Lithuanians, Mazovians, and Swedes, it has sunk into insignificance; and the modern cap., of the same name with the prov., is a town of 9,217 inhabitants in 1833, on the Bialy river, 60 m. SSW of Grodno. It is a neat, well-built place; and has a beautiful chateau, formerly appertaining to the Counts Branicki, which has been called 'the Polish Versailles.'

BIANA, or BEANA, a town of Hindostan, in the tive forest in Lithuania, in the gov. of Grodno, be- prov. and 54 m. WSW of Agra. It is situated on a tributary of the Bungunga, at the base of a hill, the summit of which is covered with the remains of the original town,-of these, the fort has recently been repaired and strengthened with additional works of masonry. Modern B. is of considerable extent, and contains many large and substantially built houses. The bazaar is commodious, and in 1820 was ex- FUTTIPUR, BETWEEN RUINS 1.00 . 隐 . - - * 2 tremely flourishing. This town was formerly the cap. of the prov., and is frequently mentioned in the history of the emperor Baber. It was first conquered by the Mahommedans in A. D. 1197. Copper and turquoise are found, and indigo is successfully cultivated in the environs.
BIANCAVILLE, a town of Sicily, in the district and 14 m. NW of Catania. Pop. 5,870.
BIANCHI MANDRI, a town of Sicily, in the intendancy and 22 m. ENE of Catania.

BIANCO, a town of Naples, in Calabria Ultra lentz. 1ma, in the district and 15 m. SSW of Gerace. Pop. 1,577.—Also a river of Naples, in the prov. of Principato Citra, rising in Monte Madalena, and flowing NW to the Sele, which it joins on the r. bank.

BIANCO (CAPE), a headland of the island of Corsica, at the NW extremity.—Also a headland of the island of Sicily in the prov. of Messina, at the extremity of the peninsula of Melazzo. - Also a headland and reef of the island of Corfu, at the S extremity. It is of considerable height, and has a lighthouse.

BIANCO CANAL, an important hydraulic work in Lombardy, commencing at the junction of the Castagnaro canal with that of Tartaro, in the Polesina, and running E to the Adriatic, into which it opens at Porto di Levante. It has a somewhat sinuous course of about 50 m. It communicates with the Po by means of the canals of Posella and Cavanella; and with the Adige, by the Loreo canal and the Adigetto. Between Adria and the sea, this canal is sometimes known as the Porto di Levante.
BIANDRATE, a town of Piedmont, in the prov. and 8 m. W of Novara. Pop. 1,800.

BIAR, a town of Spain, in the prov. and 24 m. NW of Valencia, and 4 m. E of Villena. It is situated in a fertile valley, amid mountains of calcareous formation. Some manufactories of linen and pottery exist here; and the environs afford iron and ochre. The honey of the district is renowned for the peculiar aromatic flavour which it possesses.—Also a town of Lower Egypt, on the confines of the isthmus of Suez, near Lake Tensah, on the route from Belbeis to El-Arish, and 45 m. ENE of the former.
BIARDS (LES), a commune of France, in the dep. of Manche, and cant. of Isigny. Pop. 1,164.— Also a hamlet of France, in the dep. of Haute Vienne, com. of Nixon. Pop. 98.
BIARMIA. See PERM.
BIARRITS, a commune of France, in the dep. of Basses-Pyrénées, cant. of Bayonne. It lies on the bay of Gascony, and possesses a small fishing-port and marine baths. Pop. 1,495.
BIAS BAY, an indentation of the southern coast of China, in the prov. of Quangtong, and district of Hoei-tchou.

BIASCA, a town of Switzerland, cap. of the dist. of Riviera, in the cant. of Tessin, 9 m. N of Bellinzona, on the l. bank of the Blegno, a little above the confluence of that river with the Tessin. In 1512, this town, formerly of great extent, was destroyed by a land-slip; and in 1514 it sustained a similar catastrophe. The cavities of the adjacent mountains are extensively used as wine-stores. The inhabitants of the district are liable to the goitre. Pop. 1,912.
BIAUDOS, a commune of France, in the dep. of Landes, cant. of Saint-Esprit. Pop. 803.
BIBAH, or BIBBA, a town of Central Egypt, on the l. bank of the Nile, 13 m. S of Benisuef.
BIBAN. See BEEBAN.

BIBB, a county in the central part of the state of Georgia, U. S., watered by the Ocmulgee. Area 450 sq. m. Its principal production is cotton. The cap. is Macon. Pop. 9,802.—Also a co. in the state of the duchy of Saxony, gov. of Merseburg, 6 m. N of BIB Alabama, watered by the Cahawba. Area 800 sq. m. Pop. 8,284. The cap. is Centreville.
BIBBIENA, a town of Tuscany, 25 m. N of Arezzo, near the l. bank of the Arno. Pop. 2,162. This was the birthplace of F. Bernini.
BIBBONA, a town of Tuscany, in the vicariat of Campiglia, 12 m. SW of Volterra. Pop. 658.
BIBER (Lower and Upper), two villages of Prussia, in the prov. of the Rhine, circle of Neuwied; the former 12 m., and the latter 9 m. NNW of Cob-

BIBERACH, a town of Wurtemberg, the head of an upper bailiwick in the circle of the Danube. It is situated in a fertile and agreeable valley, on the small river Riss, in the Allgau, 18 m. SSW of Ulm, at an alt. of 1,740 ft. above sea-level. The pop. is about 4,680, two-thirds of whom are Lutherans, and the rest Catholics. The town has some trade in salt, and manufactures of leather, paper, linen, woollen, and cotton stuffs, particularly fustian. B. was constituted an imperial free town at a very early period. It suffered severely in all the wars of the 17th and 18th centuries. In 1634 it was taken by the Swedes, and in 1702 by the Bavarians. The French took it in 1796; and on the 2d October following a bloody action was fought near it, between the Austrians under Latour, and the French under Moreau, in which the latter had the advantage. In 1802 the town and territory of B. were given to the elector of Baden, who ceded them in 1806 to the king of Wurtemberg. About a mile from the town is a bath called Jordansbad, which is much frequented.—Also a village in the grand duchy of Baden, circle of the Middle Rhine, on the Kinzig, SE of Offenburg. Pop. 1,207.—Also a neat market-town of Bavaria, in the circle of Suabia, or the Upper Danube, district of Wertingen, situated at the junction of the Biber and the Schmutter, 4 m. SE of Weisenhorn, and belonging formerly to the counts of Fugger. Pop. 437.
BIBERICH, or BIBRICH, a town in the grandduchy of Nassau, on the r. bank of the Rhine. Here is a splendid castle, the residence of the grand duke. The adjoining country affords excellent wine.

BIBERISCH, a small river of Saxony, which falls into the Mulda, near Nossen. - BIBERSCHLAG, a village of Germany, in that part of the principality of Cobourg which belongs to the house of Saxe-Hildburghausen. In the neighbourhood are iron works.

BIBERSTEIN, a small town of Switzerland, in the canton of Aargau, 2 m. NE of Aarau, on the l. bank of the Aar, with a castle the residence of the bailiff. It was originally the property of the counts of Habsburg. The bailiwick contains mines of iron, and produces a little wine.
BIBERT, a small river of the Bavarian dominions, in the principality of Anspach. It falls into the Rednitz, near Altenburg.
BIBERT (MARKT), a town of Bavaria, in the circle of Middle Franconia, on the Bibert, 9 m. NW of Neustadt. Pop. 570.
BIBIANA, a village of Piedmont, in the territory of Pignerol, on the river Pellice, near Lucerna. Pop. 2,800.
BIBERIBE, a river of Brazil, in the prov. of Pernambuco, which runs from W to E, and enters the Atlantic, near Olinda.
BIBLIS, a village in the grand-duchy of Hesse- Darmstadt, principality of Starkenburg, in the district of Heppenheim. Pop. 2,000.
BIBLISHEIM, or BIBELSEN, a commune of France, in the dep. of Bas-Rhin, cant. of Worth-sur-Sauer. Pop. 315. Linen thread is manufactured here.
BIBRA, or BIEBRA, a small town of Prussia, in Eckartsberga. Pop. 1,168. Here is a medicinal spring, which was first brought into notice in 1777, and is much frequented.
BIBURG, a village of Bavaria, in the circle of Lower Bavaria, 6 m. from Neustadt-on-the-Danube. Pop. 214.
BIBURY, a parish in Gloucestershire, 4 m. NW of Fairford. Area 6,300 acres. Pop. 1,077.

BIC, a small island in the river St. Lawrence, about 3 m. in length from E to W, and 3 m. in breadth. It is 153 miles below Quebec; in N lat. 48° 15'.—Also a seigniory in Lower Canada, in the co. of Rimouski, on the St. Lawrence.
BICANEER. See BIKANIR.
BICCARI, a town of Naples, in the prov. of Capitanata, 20 m. WSW of Foggia, on the E flank of the Apennines. Pop. 3,460. Important fairs are held here.
BICCIASOR, a town of Nepal, 24 m. S of Mecampur.

BICESTER, a market-town and parish of England, in the county of Oxford, on a rivulet running into the Charwell at Islip, 12 m. NNE of Oxford. Area of p. 2,580 acres. Pop. in 1801, 1,946; in 1841, 3,022.—The town is a place of some antiquity. Its chief trade arises from its large markets and cattlefairs.
BICETRE, a hamlet of France, in the dep. of Seine, cant. of Villejuif, 1 m. S of Paris. Pop. 3,500. There is an immense hospital here, which was established in 1290.
BICHAO, a river of South America, in Guayana, which falls into the Orinocco from the W, not far from the fall of Atures.
BICHARIS. See BISHARI.
BICHE, a river of North America, which falls into Lake Erie, in N lat. 42°.
BICHELSEE, a town of Switzerland, in the cant. of Thurgau, 8 m. S of Frauenfeld.
BICHIGASTA, an Indian village on the side of the Famatina range in the prov. of La Rioja in the republic of Buenos Ayres.
BICHKIN, a town of Russia, in the gov. and 30 m. SSE of Kharkov, on the l. brink of the Severnoi-Donetz.
BICHLISTAS, a town of Turkey, in the sanjak and 20 m. SW of Monastir, on a stream of the same name, a branch of the Premeritza.
BICHNI, a town of Russian Armenia, 30 m. NNE of Erivan.
BICHOLIM, a town of Hindostan, 6 m. N of Goa.
BICKENHALL, a parish in Somersetshire, 5 m. SE of Taunton. Area 1,090 acres. Pop. 264.
BICKENHILL (CHURCH), a parish in Warwickshire, 4 m. SSW of Coleshill. Area 3,810 acres. Pop. 774.
BICKENSOHL, a village of Baden, in the Upper Rhine circle, in the bailiwick of Breisach, on the Kaiserstuhl. Pop. 410.
BICKER, a parish in Lincolnshire, 9 m. NE by F of Folkingham. Area 3,720 acres. Pop. 925.

BICKERSTAFF, a township in the county of Lancaster, 4 m. SE of Ormskirk. Area 5,860 acres. Pop. 1,579.—Also a township in the p. of Malpas, Cheshire, 4 m. N of Malpas. Area 1,780 acres. Pop. 401.—Also a township in the p. of Rothbury, Northumberland.—Also a township in the p. of Bilton, in Yorkshire, 4 m. NE of Wetherby. Area 1,080 acres. Pop. 110.

BICKERTON'S ISLAND, an island in the South Pacific, called Lattai by the natives; discovered by Morello, the Spanish navigator, in 1781. It chiefly consists of a great conical mountain, the summit of which presents volcanic appearances, while the sides are covered with trees, and environed by a lower border, which is fertile and abounds in fresh water.— BID Also an island of New Holland, on the E coast of the gulf of Carpentaria, in S lat. 13° 50'. It is 10 m. from the mainland.
BICKINGTON, a parish in Devonshire, 4 m. NE of Ashburton. Area 1,220 acres. Pop. 374.
BICKINGTON (HIGH), a parish in Devonshire, 7 m. E of Great Torrington. Area 3,890 acres. Pop. 895.
BICKLEIGH, a parish in Devonshire, 3 m. SW of Tiverton, on the river Exe. Area 1,690 acres. Pop. 362.—Also a parish in Devonshire, 6 m. NNE of Plymouth. Area 2,980 acres. Pop. 469.
BICKLEY, a township in the p. of Malpas, in the co. of Chester, 4 m. from Malpas. Area 2,200 acres. Pop. 489.
BICKMERSH, a hamlet in the p. of Welford, Warwickshire, 8 m. SW of Stratford-on-Avon. Pop. with Little Dorsington, 130.
BICKNACRE, a hamlet partly in the p. of Danbury, partly in that of Woodham-Ferris, Essex. Pop. 304.
BICKNOLLER, a parish in Somersetshire, 13 m. W by N of Bridgewater. Area 1,370 acres. Pop. 345.
BICKNOR, a parish in Kent, 4 m. SW of Milton. Area 500 acres. Pop. 46.
BICKNOR-ENGLISH, a parish in Gloucestershire, 3 m. N of Colford. Area 2,440 acres. Pop. 576.
BICKNOR-WELSH, a parish in Monmouthshire, 7 m. W by S of Ross. Area 960 acres. Pop. 74.
BICOCCA, or Bicocco, called by the French La
BICOQUE, a village in the Lombardo-Venetian kingdom, 3 m. NE of Milan, near which the French, under Lautrec, were defeated by the Imperialists in 1522.
BICSKE, a town of Hungary, in the circle of Stuhl-Weissenburg, on the S flank of the Bakonywald. Pop. 3,467.
BICKTON, a chapelry in the p. of St. Chad, in Salop, 4 m. NW by W of Shrewsbury, on the Severn. Pop. 273.
BICTON, a parish in Devonshire, 4 m. WSW of Sidmouth. Area 1,180 acres. Pop. with Yettington hamlet, 198.
BICUDO, a town of Brazil, in the prov. of Minas- Geraes, on the l. bank of the Rio-das-velhas, at the junction of a small river of the same name.

BIDACHE, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Basses-Pyrénées on the river Bidouze.—The cant. comprises 8 com. Pop. in 1831, 10,505.—The com. is 16 m. E of Bayonne, and 40 m. NW of Pau. It has a strong castle. Pop. 2,640.
BIDARRAY, a commune of France, in the dep. of Basses-Pyrénées, cant. of Saint-Etienne-de-Baigony; at an alt. of 1,480 ft. above sea-level.
BIDART, a commune and small fishing-port of France in the dep. of Basses-Pyrénées, cant. of Saint-Jean-de-Luz. Pop. 804.
BIDASPES. See JAILUM.

BIDASSOA, a river which rises on the S flank of the Pyrenees; runs SW and then NW, separating the prov. of Guipuscoa in Spain, from the dep. of the Lower Pyrenees in France; and, after forming the isle of Pheasants, falls into the bay of Biscay, between Andaye and Fontarabia, after a course of 45 m. Its other appellations are the river of Andaye, Gastalabar, Vidasa, and Vidassoa. It is navigable for small vessels as far as the village of Biriaton, 6 m. from its mouth.—This river was long a subject of contest between France and Spain, each laying an exclusive claim to it; but in the 15th century Louis of France and Ferdinand I. of Spain concluded an agreement, by virtue of which the river became common to both nations. On the isle of Pheasants, at the mouth of this river, the peace of the Pyrenees, between France and Spain, was concluded in 1659.
BIDASUR, a town of India, in the Mohilla country, in the prov. and 40 m. E by S of Bikanir.
BIDBOROUGH, a parish in Kent, 3 m. SW of Tunbridge. Area 1,360 acres. Pop. 260. BIDBURG, BIEDBURG, or BIBRICH, a small town in the grand duchy of Luxemburg, 30 m. NNE of Luxemburg.
BIDDEFORD, a seaport in the state of Maine, U. S., on the S side of the Saco river, and connected with the town of Saco by a bridge. Pop. 2,574.
BIDDENDEN, a parish in Kent, 5 m. E by N of Cranbrook. Area 7,110 acres. Pop. 1,486.
BIDDENHAM, a parish in Bedfordshire, 2 m. W of Bedford. Area 1,760 acres. Pop. 345.
